past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 12 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 8 years ago, controlled with oral agents
Remote history of orthostatic dizziness evaluated 3 years ago, attributed to antihypertensive medication and dose-adjusted
family_history: Mother developed progressive memory problems in her late 70s, diagnosed with dementia, passed away at age 83
Father had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died of stroke at age 71
One older sister, age 76, reports mild forgetfulness but has not been formally evaluated

social_history.occupation: Retired seamstress, worked in a garment factory for 35 years
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband (age 76) in a two-bedroom apartment; daughter visits weekly
social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinking, less than once per month
social_history.exercise: Used to walk to the local market daily; over the past year has become less active and prefers to stay home

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place; knows the month and year but cannot state the exact date or day of the week. MMSE 21/30, losing points on recall (0/3 at 5 minutes), orientation (3 points lost), attention/calculation (2 errors on serial sevens). Clock drawing test shows numbers clustered on the right side of the clock face with hands placed at an incorrect time. Unable to recall any of 3 words after 5 minutes, even with categorical cues. Naming intact for common objects but hesitates for less frequent items. Repetition intact. Comprehension intact for simple instructions but has difficulty following a three-step command. Fluency mildly reduced for category naming.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Facial sensation intact. Face symmetric with normal strength. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout all extremities. No rigidity, no bradykinesia, no resting or postural tremor. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. Mildly decreased vibration sense at bilateral great toes, attributed to age.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign. No Babinski sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing normal. Rapid alternating movements intact. No past-pointing.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base and stride length. Tandem gait intact. Romberg sign negative. No ataxia. Turns normally without instability.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. No grasp reflex. No myoclonus. No signs of parkinsonism. Orthostatic vital signs: supine BP 138/78, standing BP 130/74 at 3 minutes — no significant orthostatic drop.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory loss and difficulty managing daily activities over the past two years
patient.history_present_illness: Mrs. Yumi Tanaka is a 73-year-old right-handed retired seamstress who presents to the outpatient neurology clinic accompanied by her daughter, Akiko, for evaluation of worsening memory difficulties over the past two years. The daughter first noticed subtle changes approximately two years ago when her mother began repeating questions during their weekly phone calls, sometimes asking the same question three or four times within a single conversation without apparent awareness of having done so. Over the following months, additional concerns emerged. Mrs. Tanaka, who had been an accomplished seamstress for decades, began making errors in sewing patterns she had known by heart for years, producing garments with misaligned seams and incorrect measurements. She also started having difficulty managing her household finances, a task she had handled independently for the entirety of her marriage. Her husband discovered several unpaid utility bills and noticed that the checkbook register contained arithmetic errors, which was highly unusual for her. Approximately one year ago, Mrs. Tanaka got lost walking home from the local market, a route she had taken daily for over twenty years, and had to call her daughter for directions. Since then, she has been reluctant to walk alone and prefers to stay in the apartment. She has forgotten to take her medications on multiple occasions, prompting her daughter to set up a pill organizer. Her cooking has also been affected; she has burned meals several times after forgetting she had left the stove on, and her husband now supervises all cooking activities. The daughter notes that her mother seems less interested in activities she previously enjoyed, including watching her favorite television programs and tending to her small balcony garden. Mrs. Tanaka acknowledges that her memory is not as good as it used to be and appears distressed about the changes, occasionally becoming tearful when discussing them. There have been no episodes of visual hallucinations, personality changes, or aggressive behavior. She has not experienced any falls, tremor, difficulty walking, seizures, or sudden onset neurological symptoms. Her sleep has been unremarkable according to her husband, with no reports of acting out dreams or excessive daytime somnolence. Three years ago, she experienced episodic dizziness upon standing that was evaluated and attributed to her antihypertensive medication; the amlodipine dose was reduced from 10 mg to 5 mg with resolution of symptoms. This prior orthostatic complaint has not recurred.

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened hippocampal fissures and enlarged choroidal fissures bilaterally, right slightly greater than left
signal_characteristics.Coronal_T1: Decreased hippocampal volume with prominent temporal horn dilatation bilaterally
Widened perihippocampal CSF spaces with thinning of the parahippocampal gyri bilaterally

findings.location: Temporoparietal cortex bilaterally, with relative frontal sparing

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened sulci in bilateral temporal and parietal convexities exceeding expected changes for age

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Scattered punctate periventricular and deep white matter hyperintensities, age-appropriate for a 73-year-old with vascular risk factors

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy, Scheltens MTA score 3 bilaterally
volumetrics.ventricular_system: Moderate lateral ventriculomegaly, particularly involving temporal horns, consistent with ex vacuo dilatation
volumetrics.cortical_thickness: Reduced cortical thickness in bilateral temporal and parietal association areas

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on diffusion-weighted imaging
No evidence of hemorrhage on susceptibility-weighted imaging
Normal appearance of the pons and cerebellum — no 'hot cross bun' sign or cerebellar atrophy
Patent major intracranial vessels on MR angiography
mri.impression: Bilateral hippocampal and medial temporal lobe atrophy (Scheltens MTA grade 3) with temporoparietal-predominant cortical volume loss disproportionate to the patient's age. The posterior fossa structures including the pons and cerebellum are normal without evidence of pontocerebellar atrophy. No acute intracranial pathology. Age-appropriate periventricular white matter changes (Fazekas grade 1). In the clinical context of progressive memory decline, the findings are most consistent with a neurodegenerative process, favoring Alzheimer disease.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Low, given severity exceeds age norms and correlates with clinical decline
Cannot be excluded by imaging alone

recommended_actions: Consider amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers for pathological confirmation of Alzheimer disease
Follow-up MRI in 12 months if clinical trajectory is uncertain

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, consistent with known type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Normal thyroid function; hypothyroid-related cognitive impairment excluded

Dementia_workup.clinical_significance: Normal; B12 deficiency not contributing to cognitive decline
Reflects fair glycemic control in known diabetic; diabetes is a risk factor for cognitive decline but does not explain the amnestic presentation

labs.interpretation: Routine dementia screening labs are essentially normal. Reversible causes of cognitive impairment including hypothyroidism, vitamin B12 deficiency, neurosyphilis, and HIV have been excluded. Glucose is mildly elevated and HbA1c at 6.8% reflects fair control of known diabetes. Complete blood count and basic metabolic panel are within normal limits. The laboratory profile supports a primary neurodegenerative etiology for the patient's progressive cognitive decline.
abnormal_values_summary: Glucose: 118 mg/dL (ref: 70-100) — consistent with known type 2 diabetes
HbA1c: 6.8% (ref: 4.0-5.6) — fair glycemic control in diabetic patient

Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Below cutoff; prominent deficits in delayed recall, visuospatial/executive, and orientation domains

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Severely impaired verbal learning and immediate memory

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall
Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Severely impaired delayed recall with rapid forgetting, hallmark of hippocampal-type amnesia
Mild anomia; low-frequency item naming impaired
Reduced semantic fluency consistent with temporal lobe dysfunction
Mildly impaired executive function and set-shifting
Borderline, suggestive of mild reactive depressive symptoms likely secondary to awareness of cognitive decline; insufficient to explain the degree of cognitive impairment

output.interpretation: Comprehensive neuropsychological testing reveals a predominantly amnestic cognitive profile consistent with early Alzheimer disease. Delayed recall is severely impaired with rapid forgetting characteristic of hippocampal-type amnesia. Semantic fluency and naming are mildly impaired, indicating temporal lobe involvement. Executive function shows mild impairment. Processing speed and visuospatial construction are relatively preserved. The GDS is borderline elevated at 5/15, likely reflecting reactive distress rather than a primary depressive disorder; the cognitive profile is inconsistent with pseudodementia. Overall pattern is consistent with multidomain amnestic mild dementia, most likely Alzheimer disease.
abnormal_values_summary: MoCA: 16/30 — below cutoff with prominent memory and orientation deficits
RAVLT Immediate Recall: 3rd percentile — severely impaired
RAVLT Delayed Recall: 1st percentile — severely impaired, hippocampal pattern
Boston Naming Test: 20th percentile — mild anomia
Trail Making Test Part B: 15th percentile — mildly impaired

findings.location: Diffuse cortical uptake, most prominent in prefrontal, precuneus, posterior cingulate, and lateral temporoparietal cortex

signal_characteristics.Distribution: Widespread bilateral cortical amyloid binding with relative sparing of primary sensorimotor and occipital cortex

additional_observations: Non-specific white matter uptake within normal limits
Cerebellar reference region demonstrates appropriate low uptake
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. Diffuse cortical amyloid deposition with elevated SUVr of 1.48, well above the positivity threshold. The distribution pattern is consistent with moderate-to-high amyloid burden typical of Alzheimer disease pathology. In the clinical context of progressive amnestic dementia and structural MRI findings, this confirms the biological diagnosis of Alzheimer disease.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Excluded by clinical context of progressive cognitive decline

recommended_actions: Integrate with clinical presentation, neuropsychological testing, and structural imaging for definitive diagnosis
Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment options with patient and family
Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy if not already started

findings.location: Bilateral temporoparietal cortex and posterior cingulate/precuneus

signal_characteristics.FDG_PET: Reduced FDG uptake in bilateral temporal and parietal association cortices with posterior cingulate and precuneus hypometabolism
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Classic AD pattern — bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism with preserved primary sensorimotor cortex, occipital cortex, basal ganglia, and cerebellum

additional_observations: Frontal lobe metabolism relatively preserved, consistent with early-stage disease
Primary visual cortex metabolism preserved, arguing against posterior cortical atrophy variant or Lewy body dementia
Basal ganglia and thalamic metabolism normal
Cerebellar metabolism normal — no pontocerebellar hypometabolism pattern
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate/precuneus hypometabolism in a pattern characteristic of Alzheimer disease. Frontal and occipital metabolism are relatively preserved, consistent with early-stage typical AD. No metabolic pattern suggestive of frontotemporal dementia or Lewy body dementia.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Very low — no anterior temporal or frontal hypometabolism

recommended_actions: Correlate with structural MRI and neuropsychological testing
Consider amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers if pathological confirmation desired

output.interpretation: CSF biomarker profile is strongly consistent with Alzheimer disease pathology. Amyloid beta-42 is significantly decreased at 348 pg/mL, and the Abeta42/40 ratio is reduced at 0.044, confirming amyloid positivity. Phosphorylated tau-181 is markedly elevated at 84 pg/mL, indicating tau-mediated neurodegeneration. Total tau is elevated at 512 pg/mL, consistent with active neuronal injury. Routine CSF parameters including cell count, protein, and glucose are within normal limits. Negative 14-3-3 protein and RT-QuIC effectively exclude prion disease. This A+T+N+ biomarker profile confirms the biological diagnosis of Alzheimer disease.

APOE_Genotyping.clinical_significance: Heterozygous APOE e4 carrier. Approximately 3-fold increased risk for late-onset Alzheimer disease. APOE e4 is the strongest common genetic risk factor for sporadic AD. This finding is consistent with but not independently diagnostic of Alzheimer disease.

output.interpretation: APOE genotyping reveals E3/E4 heterozygosity. The patient carries one copy of the APOE e4 allele, which confers an approximately 3-fold increased risk for late-onset Alzheimer disease. Given the patient's age of onset (>65 years), early-onset genetic panel testing (PSEN1, PSEN2, APP) is not clinically indicated. The APOE e4 status is a risk factor and should be interpreted in the context of clinical and biomarker findings. Genetic counseling may be offered to family members.
abnormal_values_summary: APOE Genotype: E3/E4 — heterozygous e4 carrier with increased Alzheimer disease risk

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-7.5 Hz alpha activity over occipital regions, below the normal range of 8.5-12 Hz

findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with mild-to-moderate diffuse cerebral dysfunction; typical of neurodegenerative dementias including Alzheimer disease
Bitemporal intermittent theta activity correlating with medial temporal lobe atrophy on neuroimaging

artifacts.impact: Minimal; did not obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age

output.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG. The posterior dominant rhythm is slowed at 7-7.5 Hz with intermittent bitemporal theta slowing. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. Findings are consistent with mild diffuse cerebral dysfunction and support a neurodegenerative process.
output.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed. Recording duration 30 minutes, awake state only.
recommended_actions: Correlate with structural neuroimaging and neurocognitive testing
Consider repeat EEG if clinical decline accelerates, to evaluate for rapidly progressive dementia etiologies

interactions: No significant interactions with amlodipine, metformin, rosuvastatin, or alendronate
warnings: May cause bradycardia; baseline HR 72 bpm — monitor heart rate, particularly in combination with amlodipine
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) are common at initiation, usually transient; monitor in context of concurrent metformin which may also cause GI symptoms
Avoid concurrent use of anticholinergic medications as they may reduce donepezil efficacy
alternatives: Rivastigmine (patch or oral) — alternative cholinesterase inhibitor, may have fewer GI side effects with transdermal formulation

results.title: 2023 AAN Practice Guideline: Mild Cognitive Impairment and Early Alzheimer Disease
results.summary: Recommends cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) as first-line for mild-to-moderate AD. Memantine may be added at moderate stage. Anti-amyloid therapies (lecanemab, donanemab) may be considered for early AD with confirmed amyloid pathology in appropriate candidates.

results.title: Appropriate use recommendations for lecanemab and donanemab in Alzheimer disease
results.summary: Anti-amyloid antibodies require confirmed amyloid positivity (PET or CSF), MCI or mild dementia stage, APOE genotyping (e4 homozygotes at higher ARIA risk), baseline MRI screening for microbleeds. Require regular MRI monitoring for ARIA.

results.title: Non-pharmacological interventions in early Alzheimer disease
results.summary: Evidence supports cognitive stimulation therapy, physical exercise programs, occupational therapy, and caregiver education. Combined pharmacological and non-pharmacological approaches yield best outcomes.

output.summary: Current guidelines recommend cholinesterase inhibitors as first-line pharmacotherapy for early AD. Anti-amyloid antibodies (lecanemab, donanemab) are emerging options for patients with confirmed amyloid pathology. Non-pharmacological interventions including cognitive stimulation and exercise should be combined with pharmacotherapy. Comprehensive caregiver support and safety planning are essential components of management.

Autonomic_Function_Testing.clinical_significance: No orthostatic hypotension. BP drop of 8/4 mmHg is within normal limits.
No evidence of cardiac autonomic dysfunction

output.interpretation: Autonomic function testing is within normal limits. No orthostatic hypotension is demonstrated on tilt-table testing. Heart rate variability is normal for age. These findings argue against a synucleinopathy with autonomic involvement such as multiple system atrophy, dementia with Lewy bodies, or pure autonomic failure. The absence of autonomic dysfunction is consistent with early Alzheimer disease.

differential.key_distinguishing: MMSE 21/30 is well below normal range; neuropsychological testing confirms severe memory impairment; clear functional decline documented by family

Gradual progressive decline rather than stepwise; memory-predominant rather than executive-predominant pattern; MRI shows only Fazekas grade 1 white matter changes despite vascular risk factors; no acute or subacute infarcts

Insidious onset over 2 years; GDS 5/15 shows only borderline depressive symptoms likely reactive; cognitive deficits disproportionate to mood symptoms; neuropsych profile shows hippocampal-type amnesia inconsistent with effort-dependent poor performance
No visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism on exam, no REM sleep behavior disorder reported, no fluctuating cognition; neurological exam shows no rigidity, bradykinesia, or tremor
No personality or behavioral changes; memory impairment is the predominant symptom; social decorum preserved; no disinhibition or apathy beyond what is attributable to cognitive decline

No cerebellar ataxia, no gait instability, no dysarthria, no orthostatic hypotension on current testing, no cerebellar atrophy or pontine abnormalities on MRI; cognitive profile is amnestic rather than cerebellar/subcortical

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and informant, including timeline of cognitive decline, nature of memory complaints, and functional status changes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive amnestic decline over 2 years with multiple domains of functional impairment confirmed by daughter

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including detailed mental status assessment with MMSE or MoCA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: MMSE 21/30 with prominent recall deficit (0/3); neurological exam otherwise unremarkable with no cerebellar, parkinsonian, or motor findings

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening labs: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, HIV
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All within normal limits except mildly elevated glucose and HbA1c consistent with known diabetes; reversible causes of dementia excluded

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 3) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss; normal posterior fossa structures
Amnestic profile with severely impaired delayed recall (hippocampal-type amnesia), reduced semantic fluency, mild executive impairment; consistent with early Alzheimer disease

optimal_actions.action: Consider CSF biomarkers or amyloid PET for pathological confirmation of Alzheimer disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Decreased Abeta42, elevated phospho-tau and total tau confirming AD pathology (A+T+N+)

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating cholinesterase inhibitor therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant interactions with current medications

optimal_actions.action: Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily)
Provide diagnosis disclosure and counseling to patient and family, including prognosis, safety planning, advance directives, and community resources
Optimize vascular risk factor management (diabetes and hypertension control) as part of comprehensive dementia care
Schedule follow-up in 3-6 months to assess treatment response and disease progression

critical_actions: Exclude reversible causes of dementia with laboratory screening (TSH, B12, RPR, HIV)
Perform standardized cognitive assessment (MMSE, MoCA, or neuropsychological battery)
Obtain collateral history from family member or informant to confirm cognitive and functional decline
Assess functional status and safety (medication management, cooking, navigation, finances)
Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and safety planning with patient and family
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., diphenhydramine, oxybutynin) which worsen cognitive function in dementia
Prescribing benzodiazepines for anxiety without careful consideration of cognitive side effects and fall risk
Attributing progressive cognitive decline to normal aging without formal evaluation
Starting memantine monotherapy in mild disease (not indicated until moderate stage)
Ordering brain biopsy (not indicated given clinical and biomarker profile)
Prescribing antipsychotics without clear behavioral indication (risk of increased mortality in dementia patients)
Discontinuing diabetes medications without endocrine consultation, as uncontrolled diabetes may accelerate cognitive decline
key_reasoning_points: Progressive episodic memory loss over 2 years with insidious onset and gradual worsening is the hallmark presentation of early Alzheimer disease
Multiple domains of functional decline (finances, cooking, navigation, medication management) distinguish dementia from mild cognitive impairment
MMSE 21/30 with disproportionate recall deficit (0/3 at 5 minutes) is characteristic of the hippocampal-type amnesia seen in Alzheimer disease
Normal neurological exam aside from cognitive deficits — no cerebellar signs, no parkinsonism, no motor deficits — argues against MSA, Lewy body dementia, and vascular dementia

The source case's original MSA diagnosis is excluded by the absence of cerebellar ataxia, gait instability, dysarthria, and orthostatic hypotension; normal posterior fossa on MRI without hot cross bun sign or cerebellar atrophy
Family history of maternal dementia is a significant risk factor supporting Alzheimer disease
MRI showing bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 3) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss and normal posterior fossa structures is the classic neuroimaging pattern for Alzheimer disease
Normal dementia screening labs rule out treatable causes (hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, neurosyphilis, HIV) and support a primary neurodegenerative diagnosis
Vascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes) are present but MRI shows only minimal white matter disease (Fazekas 1), making vascular dementia unlikely as the primary etiology
Prior orthostatic dizziness was medication-related and resolved with dose adjustment, distinguishing it from the autonomic dysfunction seen in synucleinopathies

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic amnestic presentation, supportive family history, characteristic MRI findings of hippocampal atrophy, and absence of features suggesting alternative diagnoses. The clinical picture clearly points to Alzheimer disease with minimal confounders.
metadata.adaptation_notes: Clinical foundation inspired by published MSA case. Patient demographics modified to fit typical AD presentation (age increased to 73, cerebellar/autonomic features replaced with progressive amnestic syndrome). The source case's orthostatic dizziness is preserved as a historical finding attributed to medication, and the normal posterior fossa structures on MRI explicitly exclude MSA.
